description: Salesforce Certified MuleSoft Developer II mock exam. Advanced error handling, batch processing, security (OAuth/JWT/TLS), API governance, CloudHub/RTF deployment, and MuleSoft architecture patterns. Managed via Salesforce Webassessor/Trailhead since May 2024. Exam conditions: no feedback until the end.

questions.q: Which error handler type continues flow execution after handling the error?
questions.explanation: on-error-continue suppresses the error and continues execution; propagate re-raises it.

questions.q: An on-error-propagate inside a Try scope catches a VALIDATION error. After the handler runs, what happens?
options: Flow resumes after the Try scope
The error is re-raised to the parent error handler
questions.explanation: propagate always re-raises — the error bubbles to the next handler up the chain.

questions.q: A global error handler is defined and a flow has its own local error handler. An error is raised in that flow. Which handler processes it?
options: Local handler runs first; global only if local doesn't match
Neither — the error propagates to the source
questions.explanation: Mule error handling is hierarchical: local → parent scope → flow → global.

questions.q: The error.cause attribute gives you:
options: The payload at time of error
questions.explanation: error.cause is the original Java Throwable; error.errorType gives the Mule typed identifier.

questions.q: You want to handle only APP:ORDER_NOT_FOUND but not any other APP errors. The correct on-error-propagate configuration is:
questions.explanation: Specify the full namespace:identifier in the type attribute for targeted matching.

questions.q: Which component raises a custom Mule error from application logic?
questions.explanation: Raise Error creates typed errors; type must be in APP namespace or a custom namespace.

questions.q: In a Scatter-Gather, one route fails. By default:
options: The entire Scatter-Gather fails with a MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING error
Failed routes are skipped; others complete
questions.explanation: Any route failure in Scatter-Gather raises MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING containing all route errors.

questions.q: What is the primary trigger for a Batch Job?
options: Any Mule event passed to Batch Job Trigger
questions.explanation: Batch Job Trigger accepts any upstream event and begins a batch instance.

questions.q: The Batch Step's acceptExpression attribute controls:
questions.explanation: acceptExpression is a DataWeave Boolean that gates each record into the step.

questions.q: What does the maxFailedRecords attribute on Batch Job do?
options: Stops the batch when failed record count exceeds the value
questions.explanation: maxFailedRecords=-1 means no limit; any positive integer stops the job early when exceeded.

questions.q: In the Batch Aggregator scope, the variables payload and $$record refer to:
options: The full dataset and a single record
A chunk of records and the index
All processed records and remaining records
An array of records in the chunk and no $$ is available
questions.explanation: Inside Batch Aggregator, payload is the current chunk array; $$ is not a special variable there.

questions.q: Which phase of batch processing is where you query your source system and split the result?
options: Input phase / the flow before Batch Trigger
questions.explanation: Loading/splitting data happens before the Batch Job; the job processes pre-split records.

questions.q: A batch job processes 10 000 records with a commit interval of 100. How many database commits occur in a step with a DB Insert?
questions.explanation: Records are grouped into chunks of commit-interval size; each chunk commits once.

questions.q: Which grant type is appropriate for a backend-to-backend API call where no user is involved?
questions.explanation: Client Credentials is designed for machine-to-machine; no user consent flow needed.

questions.q: A JWT is validated by the JWT policy. Where is the signature verified?
options: By API Manager against a JWKS or shared secret
questions.explanation: API Manager's JWT policy uses the configured JWKS URI or secret to verify the token signature.

questions.q: Which Anypoint component stores and provides runtime access to secrets such as keystore passwords?
questions.explanation: Anypoint Secrets Manager provides secure secret retrieval at runtime via the Secure Properties connector.

questions.q: mTLS differs from standard TLS in that:
options: The client also presents a certificate for mutual authentication
questions.explanation: mTLS (mutual TLS) requires both server and client to authenticate with certificates.

questions.q: The Client ID Enforcement policy applied to an API requires consumers to send:
options: client_id and client_secret as headers or query params
An API key in the X-API-Key header only
questions.explanation: Client ID Enforcement validates the client_id/client_secret pair against the registered application.

questions.q: Secure Properties (${secure::prop}) decrypt at:
options: Runtime startup when the app initializes
questions.explanation: The Secure Properties Tool decrypts property values during application initialization.

questions.q: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow — where does the redirect_uri go after the user authenticates?
options: To the authorization server to fetch the token
To the client application's registered callback URL
questions.explanation: After auth, the AS redirects to the client's registered redirect_uri with the code.

questions.q: TLS context in a Mule app stores the keystore for:
options: Outbound client authentication and/or inbound server TLS termination
questions.explanation: TLS context covers both the server cert (listener) and client cert (requests), depending on configuration.

questions.q: Autodiscovery wires your deployed Mule app to API Manager. The required configuration is:
options: The API ID and environment credentials injected at deploy time
questions.explanation: Autodiscovery needs the API ID (from API Manager) and platform credentials to register the runtime.

questions.q: An API policy applied in API Manager takes effect:
questions.explanation: Policies are applied dynamically via the agent; no redeploy needed.

questions.q: SLA-based rate limiting in API Manager requires consumers to:
options: Manually configure throttling in their app
Request access at an SLA tier that sets their rate limit
questions.explanation: SLA tiers define rate limits; consumer apps request access and API Manager enforces per client_id.

questions.q: Which policy type passes analytics data to Anypoint Platform without blocking requests?
questions.explanation: Message Logging/analytics policies are non-blocking observers; threat protection and rate limiting can block.

questions.q: API Versioning — when you publish v2 of an API, the recommended approach for v1 consumers is:
options: Maintain v1 alongside v2 with a deprecation notice, using Exchange to communicate sunset date
Force consumers to upgrade within a week
questions.explanation: Anypoint Exchange supports marking versions as deprecated; maintain both during the transition period.

questions.q: Contract-first API development means:
options: Writing the spec (RAML/OAS) before any implementation
questions.explanation: Contract-first: spec is the source of truth; stubs/mocks let consumers and producers work in parallel.

questions.q: CloudHub 1.0 workers run in:
options: Isolated JVMs, one per worker, on dedicated cloud infrastructure
questions.explanation: CloudHub 1.0 workers are isolated JVM processes on MuleSoft-managed infrastructure.

questions.q: CloudHub 2.0 differs from 1.0 primarily because it:
options: Uses a different Mule runtime version
Runs on Kubernetes (Anypoint Runtime Fabric hosted by MuleSoft)
questions.explanation: CH2 is built on Kubernetes/RTF; apps run in containers giving better resource efficiency and scaling.

questions.q: Runtime Fabric (RTF) on-prem lets you:
options: Deploy MuleSoft apps to your own Kubernetes cluster managed by Anypoint Platform
Run apps without a control plane connection
questions.explanation: RTF extends Anypoint control plane management to customer-managed Kubernetes clusters.

questions.q: An app on CloudHub 1.0 with 2 workers and static IP enabled — each worker gets:
questions.explanation: Each worker gets its own static IP in CloudHub 1.0 when static IPs are enabled.

questions.q: Persistent queues in CloudHub enable:
options: Message durability — inflight messages survive worker restarts
questions.explanation: Persistent queues back VM queues to disk so messages survive worker crashes/restarts.

questions.q: The Mule Maven Plugin goal to deploy to CloudHub is:
questions.explanation: `mule:deploy` triggers the deployment; `mule:package` creates the deployable artifact.

questions.q: Which Runtime Manager feature lets you run multiple Mule apps on a single on-prem Mule runtime?
questions.explanation: Mule domains share resources (connections, error handlers) among co-deployed apps on the same runtime.

questions.q: For zero-downtime deployments on CloudHub 1.0, you should enable:
questions.explanation: Rolling update (default) replaces workers incrementally; recreate stops all workers first.

questions.q: A flow has no error handler. An HTTP listener receives a failed event. What response does the caller get?
options: 500 with the default Mule error response
questions.explanation: Without an error handler, the default handler returns 500 to the caller.

questions.q: Which DataWeave function retrieves error details inside an error handler?
questions.explanation: The error object exposes errorType, description, cause, and more inside handler scopes.

questions.q: The On Complete phase of a Batch Job receives:
options: A BatchJobResult summary with success/failure counts
questions.explanation: On Complete receives a BatchJobResult payload with totalItems, successfulItems, failedItems counts.

questions.q: Batch processing uses a persistent Object Store by default to:
options: Track record processing state for restart capability
questions.explanation: Object Store persistence enables batch jobs to resume after failure from the last commit boundary.

questions.q: A refresh token is used to:
options: Re-authenticate the user with their password
Obtain a new access token without requiring user re-authentication
questions.explanation: Refresh tokens are long-lived credentials for obtaining new access tokens after expiry.

questions.q: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) is used in Authorization Code flow to protect against:
options: Authorization code interception attacks (especially in public clients)
questions.explanation: PKCE adds a code_verifier/code_challenge pair to prevent stolen authorization codes from being exchanged.

questions.q: The OAuth 2.0 scope parameter is used to:
options: Request specific permissions the access token should grant
questions.explanation: Scopes define the access level the client requests; the AS grants a subset if appropriate.

questions.q: API Analytics in Anypoint Platform tracks:
options: Request counts, response times, error rates, and policy violations per API
questions.explanation: API Analytics dashboards show detailed per-API traffic metrics sent by the runtime agent.

questions.q: A policy with 'order: 1' vs 'order: 2' — which executes first on a request?
options: Order 1 (lower number = higher priority = executes first)
questions.explanation: Policies execute in ascending order number on the request side; order reverses on the response side.

questions.q: Anypoint Exchange serves as:
options: The single source of truth for API specs, connectors, templates, examples, and documentation across the organization
questions.explanation: Exchange is the central catalog for all reusable Anypoint assets across business groups.

questions.q: A Mule cluster with two nodes — how are requests distributed?
options: By a load balancer across both nodes; VM queues are shared
One node is active, one is standby
questions.explanation: Cluster nodes share VM queues and work together; a load balancer distributes inbound requests.

questions.q: Object Store v2 on CloudHub is:
options: Deprecated and replaced by VM queues
A fully managed, persistent, distributed key-value store replicated across the region
Stored in the app's local filesystem
questions.explanation: OS v2 provides durable, shared state accessible across workers and worker restarts.

questions.q: A connector throws CONNECTIVITY:TIMEOUT. To retry automatically, you should use:
questions.explanation: Until Successful retries the enclosed processors on any error up to a configured max retry count.

questions.q: Until Successful exhausts all retries without success. The error raised is:
questions.explanation: MULE:RETRY_EXHAUSTED wraps the underlying cause when all retries are spent.

questions.q: To process records in a Batch Job with variable-length parallel threads, you configure:
questions.explanation: maxConcurrency controls how many threads process records in parallel; schedulingStrategy can pin threads.

questions.q: A Secure Properties Config requires a key at deploy time. Best practice for injecting this key on CloudHub is:
options: Pass as a deployment property (encrypted) via Runtime Manager / Anypoint CLI
Put it in a non-secured properties file
questions.explanation: The encryption key should be injected as a runtime property, not stored in the codebase.

questions.q: Which API Manager entity represents a deployed API instance that can have policies applied?
questions.explanation: API Instances in API Manager are the policy-enforceable entities linked to a deployed runtime app via autodiscovery.

questions.q: What does enabling 'Enhanced Security' on a CloudHub worker do?
options: Runs the worker in an isolated VPC with restricted egress
questions.explanation: Enhanced Security mode runs workers in dedicated VPCs with stricter network isolation.

questions.q: error.errorType.namespace for a connector error like HTTP:UNAUTHORIZED is:
questions.explanation: Connector-defined error types use the connector's namespace (HTTP, DB, JMS, etc.).

questions.q: Which batch configuration attribute controls how many records are accumulated before a step commits?
questions.explanation: batchBlockSize (also called commit interval in Studio) sets how many records per chunk before committing.

questions.q: Which Mule component validates an inbound JWT without calling an external identity provider at runtime?
options: OAuth 2.0 Access Token Enforcement policy calling the IDP
JWT Validation policy using a pre-configured JWKS URI or shared secret
questions.explanation: JWT policy verifies signature locally using the JWKS keys cached from the JWKS URI.

questions.q: Automated policy promotion from sandbox to production in Anypoint is achieved using:
options: Manual copy in API Manager UI
Anypoint CLI or Platform API to script policy configurations per environment
questions.explanation: Use the Anypoint Platform API or CLI to script policy promotion across environments as part of CI/CD.

questions.q: When deploying to a server group in Runtime Manager, requests are:
options: Handled by a single designated primary node
Load-balanced across all healthy servers in the group
questions.explanation: Server groups distribute load; if a server goes down, others handle requests.

questions.q: A Batch Job's record-level error is accessible via:
options: error in an on-error handler inside the batch step
questions.explanation: Wrap processors in a Try scope inside the batch step; on-error-continue there handles per-record errors.

questions.q: The primary difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) is:
options: OIDC adds an ID token and UserInfo endpoint on top of OAuth 2.0 for user identity
questions.explanation: OIDC is an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 — OAuth handles authorization; OIDC handles authentication.

questions.q: The minimum recommended configuration for a production CloudHub 1.0 app handling consistent load is:
options: At least 1 vCore with ≥2 workers for HA and rolling updates
Any size with persistent queues only
questions.explanation: ≥2 workers enables rolling deployment and ensures availability during restarts; vCore sizing depends on load.

questions.q: An 'Automated policy' in API Governance (Anypoint Governance) differs from a regular API Manager policy because it:
options: Is applied organization-wide via rulesets without touching individual API Manager instances
questions.explanation: Anypoint Governance rulesets enforce standards automatically across all Exchange assets in an org/BG.
